title: Linda Adade
text:
I am a Master’s student in Geography at the University of Oregon. My research is driven by an interest in understanding why development unfolds unevenly across places and how global economic processes shape opportunities for growth and transformation. I am particularly interested in the ways infrastructure, investment, and broader patterns of economic change reshape regions, cities, and communities.
My current research examines the spatial distribution of Chinese-financed infrastructure projects across Africa. Through GIS and spatial analysis, I explore how patterns of investment vary across regions and what they reveal about broader processes of development, connectivity, and spatial inequality.
Before joining the University of Oregon, I earned Bachelor’s degrees in Geography and Resource Development and Sociology from the University of Ghana. My broader interests lie in urban and economic geography, development, China-Africa relations, and the changing geographies of Africa.

title: Population and Landscape Change in the Denver Metro Area
text: This study examines demographic change and spatial inequality across the Denver–Aurora–Lakewood Metropolitan Statistical Area. With a population of nearly 3 million residents, Denver is one of the fastest-growing metropolitan regions in the United States and represents more than half of Colorado’s population. As the region has expanded, demographic and socioeconomic differences have emerged across urban, suburban, and exurban landscapes. Using data from the 2020 Decennial Census and the 2016–2020 American Community Survey (ACS), census tracts are classified into five landscape types: urban high, urban low, suburban high, suburban low, and exurban, using population density thresholds adapted from Hanberry (2022). This framework allows demographic patterns to be examined across a continuous urban–rural gradient rather than relying solely on administrative boundaries. The analysis explores how income, poverty, racial composition, and age structure vary across these landscape types and what these patterns reveal about metropolitan growth in Denver.

title: Population and Landscape Change in the Denver Metro Area
text:
About Population and Landscape Change in the Denver Metro Area


MyNorfolk is a service that allows residents of the City of Norfolk to request services online from their computer or mobile device. The data used in this dashboard is available on the Norfolk Open Data Portal.

This analysis examines demographic change and spatial inequality across the Denver -Aurora Metropolitan Statistical Area. Using 2020 Decennial Census and 2016 - 2020 American Community Survey (ACS) data, I classify census tracts by population density into urban, suburban, and exurban landscapes and ask: how do income, poverty, racial composition, and age structure vary across these landscape types - and what does that variation reveal about who benefits from Denver’s growth? Census tracts are classified using density thresholds drawn from Hanberry (2022), which distinguishes urban, suburban, and exurban landscapes based on persons per square kilometer. This framework provides a systematic basis for comparing demographic patterns across landscape types rather than relying on administrative boundaries alone.

title: Population and Landscape Change in the Denver Metro Area
text: This study examines demographic change and spatial inequality across the Denver–Aurora–Lakewood Metropolitan Statistical Area. With a population of nearly 3 million residents, Denver is one of the fastest-growing metropolitan regions in the United States and represents more than half of Colorado’s population. As the region has expanded, demographic and socioeconomic differences have emerged across urban, suburban, and exurban landscapes. Using data from the 2020 Decennial Census and the 2016–2020 American Community Survey (ACS), census tracts are classified into five landscape types: urban high, urban low, suburban high, suburban low, and exurban, using population density thresholds adapted from Hanberry (2022). This framework allows demographic patterns to be examined across a continuous urban–rural gradient rather than relying solely on administrative boundaries. The analysis explores how income, poverty, racial composition, and age structure vary across these landscape types and what these patterns reveal about metropolitan growth in Denver.

title: Population and Landscape Change in the Denver Metro Area
text:
Landscape Classification
Urban high-density tracts concentrate in the city core while suburban and exurban tracts extend toward the periphery. Although exurban areas occupy the largest share of land, most residents live within the urban core, illustrating the uneven relationship between population concentration and land area.

title: Population and Landscape Change in the Denver Metro Area
text:
Income
Median household income increases from urban to exurban landscapes, suggesting that higher-income households are concentrated in lower-density parts of the metropolitan region

title: Population and Landscape Change in the Denver Metro Area
text:
Poverty
Poverty is most concentrated in the urban core, particularly central Denver. As you move outward from the core, poverty rates generally decline, with much of the suburban ring showing lower poverty rates. However, the pattern is not uniform; there are still scattered pockets of higher poverty in outer and peripheral areas, including parts of the southwest metro and more exurban tracts. Overall, Denver shows a strong urban concentration of poverty, combined with a more uneven and dispersed poverty pattern outside the core, rather than a smooth decline.

title: Population and Landscape Change in the Denver Metro Area
text:
Racial Composition
Hispanic residents are concentrated in urban and inner suburban areas, where they represent the largest minority populations across the metropolitan region. In contrast, suburban and exurban landscapes remain majority White, reinforcing a persistent spatial gradient in racial composition across metropolitan space.

title: Population and Landscape Change in the Denver Metro Area
text:
Age Structure
Urban tracts are characterized by a younger population structure, suburban tracts by a concentration of family households and working-age adults, and exurban tracts by an older population profile.

title: Population and Landscape Change in the Denver Metro Area
text:
What the Data Reveal
Overall, the findings highlight a consistent spatial gradient in which socioeconomic status increases with decreasing density. However, this relationship is mediated by housing tenure, life stage, and racial composition, suggesting that metropolitan growth in Denver is unevenly distributed across both space and social groups.
